segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The Deep Hall breathed.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: It wasn't sound so much as pulse -- a slow press and release through the pillars as if the mountain remembered tides. The chalk sigils held their cold stars at the thresholds; the lichen dimmed and brightened with a rhythm that matched no heartbeat the living could claim.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana took first watch alone.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik was meant to be with her, but he'd sat down to adjust a lens housing and was asleep inside a minute, chin on chest, snoring with the determined regularity of a man who'd learned to sleep anywhere and wasn't about to let consciousness get a word in.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She let him. He'd earned the rest. And she needed the quiet for what she had to do.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She walked the perimeter, counting pillars, counting breaths. The chalk marks held. The lichen pulsed. Everything was steady, and the steadiness was worse than noise because it left room for the question.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: At the threshold of the Divination alcove, she stopped.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She'd been avoiding this for days -- testing it in fragments, whispered half-prayers, fingers against the Silver Dawn symbol at her throat in the dark where no one could see the light fail to answer.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: But here, in a place where the Weave still remembered what whole felt like, she had no more excuses.

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "Guide me,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she whispered.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The reply came in a forked whisper that wasn't wind.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Shield the bearer.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Offer the bearer.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Two instructions. One voice. The same voice -- she was nearly certain now -- split down a seam she couldn't find. The first time she'd heard it, at the crater's rim, she'd thought it was her own doubt echoing. The second time, exhaustion.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The third time, the forest road, she'd almost convinced herself it was the trees.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: This was the fourth time, and the Deep Hall's wards stripped away every excuse. The words came through the stone's honest geometry with terrible clarity.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Shield him or sacrifice him. Which instruction was the god? Which was the thing pressing up from below? Or -- and this was the thought she couldn't put down, the one that sat in her chest like a swallowed coal -- were they the same voice?

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Had her god always spoken in contradictions, and she'd simply been too faithful to notice?

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Her chalk snapped in her fist. Thornik shifted in his sleep; she kept her face neutral, thumb dusting grit from her glove as if that had always been the plan.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The ward line brightened. The geometry held.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Her faith did not.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She looked at her palm -- white dust in the lines of her hand, the creases mapped like roads she'd been traveling her whole life -- and thought: I have been talking to something for fifteen years. I still don't know its name. And it might have been two things all along.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana's hand went to the sword; Thornik's -- stirring now, one eye open, the instinct to guard waking before the rest of him -- to the little hammer at his belt. Nothing came. The silence was just silence. The mountain was just mountain.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She didn't tell him what she'd heard. She would. But not tonight.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Second watch: Vex and Caelin.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They sat with their backs to opposite sides of a pillar, daggers and dragonfire keeping each other honest. Vex sharpened a blade with habitual precision -- the scrape-scrape metronome, steady as a heartbeat she could trust.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin balanced Nyxara's black coin an inch above his palm, a column of warm air on one side, a whisper of cold on the other. The spiral held for three seconds this time before collapsing. Progress. Possibly.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Your shoulder?"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he asked without looking.

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "Attached,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Functional. I complain later."

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "You could let Thornik rebind it."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex made a face in the dark and then softened it because there was no one to see.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "He fusses."

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "He cares."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "That's worse."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They sat a while longer, the coin circling in the slow orbit of the too-careful, the scrape-scrape of steel on stone marking seconds. The lichen pulsed. The mountain breathed.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "My mother's notes stop mid-sentence,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex said eventually, the words landing like she'd been carrying them for hours and decided the weight wasn't worth the privacy.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Page seventy-three. Last journal. She was writing about fire-kept covenants -- the old alliances, the pacts between dragons and dwarves -- and mid-word, the ink just stops. Not a period. Not a dash. The pen lifted and never came back down."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin let the coin fall. It seemed wrong to hold magic while she was holding this.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Four hundred times,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she continued, the blade still moving because her hands needed something to do that wasn't feeling.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "I've read that page four hundred times. I know the pressure of her hand from the ink depth. She was writing fast -- excited, I think, not afraid. She'd found something. Connected something. And then -- "

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Gone."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Gone."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Scrape-scrape.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Three weeks before the dragon started burning villages. Sometimes I think she found what Vharisax was looking for. Found it first. And that's what killed her."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She looked at him, mismatched eyes catching the scale's coal-glow.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "And now I'm following the same dead-end sentence with the same dead-end questions, and the only map I have is a woman who couldn't finish writing the word covenant."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I think she'd be proud of you,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin said.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The scale stung -- a pinprick, gentle. Not lie, but not the whole truth. He corrected:

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "I think she'd be terrified for you. And proud. Both at once."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The sting faded. Truth accepted.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Finally Vex said, low:

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "You do know this ends badly, yes? The relics. The Seal. Threads like these don't tie themselves into bows."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "I know."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin didn't let the coin fall this time.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I don't know what it looks like when it ends. But I know it isn't clean."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "That's the first sensible thing you've said since Thornwick."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She breathed out, not quite a laugh.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Keep saying sensible things, Ember-boy. It helps."
